**trapezoidal rule** — the page-12 log-tables formula for estimating the area of a region using ordinate heights at equal strip widths
finite geometric series — applied to **`n`-th roots of unity**. The key collapse: `ωⁿ = 1` makes the numerator of the `Sₙ` formula equal to zero
**annuity / amortisation** — find the level monthly repayment that pays off a loan exactly over a fixed term, given the principal and the monthly interest rate
**independent events** — the throws are independent (the dice have no memory), so probabilities multiply
**p-value** — the probability of observing a sample mean at least as extreme as the one we got, **assuming `H₀` is true**
**angle bisector locus** — a point is on an angle bisector of two lines iff it is **equidistant** from both lines. Two angle bisectors (perpendicular to each other) give two cases: `+` and `−`

**internally touching circles** — when two circles touch internally, their two centres and the touch point are **collinear**, with the smaller circle's centre between the larger centre and the touch point. The distance between the two centres equals the **difference** of the radii
**Construction 21** from the LCHL prescribed-constructions list. The **centroid** is the intersection of the three **medians** (each median joins a vertex to the **midpoint** of the opposite side)
**right-triangle trigonometry** — the half-angle from Joan to a tangent point on the circular green

induction (divisibility type) — the split-the-coefficient template

**inverting** the fractional-power formula — given `y`, solve for `x`. Uses **logs** to extract `x` from a fractional power
apply the **Fibonacci recurrence** — each term is the sum of the two preceding terms
**Binet's closed-form formula** for the Fibonacci numbers — substitute `n = 3`, expand the cubes, simplify
enumeration — list every combination of hits/misses that satisfies "at least two hits"
**95% confidence interval** for a population mean — `x̄ ± 1.96 · σ/√n`
integer power of a complex number via De Moivre's Theorem. Method is prescribed by the question ("Use De Moivre's Theorem")
sketching the **exponential-decay** function `e^{−x}` — the reflection of `e^x` in the y-axis. Anchor values and shape
percentage of a fixed amount

solve the amortisation formula for `t` given `A`, `P`, and `i`. Algebra to isolate the term with the unknown in the exponent, then take `log`
**three-point method** for the equation of a circle. Plug each point into the general form `x² + y² + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0` to get three linear equations in `g`, `f`, `c`. Solve
**ratio of corresponding sides in similar triangles** — once the correspondence is locked in, write the ratio equation and solve

**area by inclusion-exclusion** on overlapping triangles cut out of the rectangle. The quadrilateral `GCDE` is what remains of the rectangle after removing `△AFD` and `△AGB` (and adding back the overlap `△AFE`, which gets subtracted twice)

**two-tailed hypothesis test** for a population mean using the **Central Limit Theorem** + **z-test** (page 36 critical value `±1·96` for two-tailed 5%). Load-bearing alternative: build a **95% confidence interval** for the population mean and check whether `63·5` is inside
apply trigonometry to a **second** right-angled triangle (`EDT`), then use **Pythagoras** in the horizontal plane to express `|DT|` in terms of `|CT|`
apply De Moivre's Theorem (just proved in part (a)) with `n = 3` to a specific complex number. The given number is one of the **complex cube roots of unity** — `z³ = 1` is the punchline

**intersection of two curves** — set them equal, rearrange to zero, factor and solve
**graph of an inverse function** — by reflection in the line `y = x`. Since the diagram already contains `h(x) = x`, we have the axis of reflection drawn
**evaluate `t(x)` at a grid of points** and plot
**max/min via differentiation** — the Differentiation 15 recipe applied to `h(x)`. Requires the **chain rule for `ln`** (Differentiation 9)
**`Tₙ` of a geometric sequence** — page-22 log-tables formula `Tₙ = a · r^(n−1)`, or by direct inspection
**circle with a tangent line at a known point** + **two touching circles centred on the line of centres**
**prescribed proof of Theorem 12** — one of the three required LCHL geometry proofs
use part (a)'s factorisation with the substitution `y → ln x`; then convert a log equation to index form to solve; check the domain of `ln`
integrate the derivative `f'(x)` to recover `f(x)` up to a constant `C`; then use the **point condition** (the graph passes through `A(2, 0)`, i.e. `f(2) = 0`) to pin down `C`
evaluate `r'(30)` and show it's **positive** — the sign of the first derivative is the test for increasing/decreasing
slope of a straight line — `m = Δy / Δx` between two known points, or read off as the coefficient of `x` when the line is written as `y = mx + c`

building successive triangular numbers using the recursive `Tₙ = Tₙ₋₁ + n`

divide a line segment in a given ratio, then compute a length

the **centres-collinear theorem** for externally touching circles — when two circles touch (externally or internally), the **point of contact lies on the line joining the centres**. So all circles touching `c` at the same point have centres on the **same line through `c`'s centre and the point of contact**
**two-tail probability** for a normal distribution — standardise both endpoints, find `P(z₁ ≤ Z ≤ z₂)` from the tables
verify a point lies on a line by substituting into the line equation
perpendicular slopes — product = `−1`. Or equivalently: `AB · AP = 0` (dot product of vectors at the vertex)
**prescribed Strand 3 construction** — `√3` via two overlapping circles (the "vesica piscis" construction). Compass and ruler only

existence of an inverse function — depends on whether the original function is **injective** (one-to-one) on the given domain

volume of a cone — `V = (1/3) π r² h` (log-tables page 10)
indefinite integral of a polynomial — apply the **reverse power rule** term by term
modulus of a complex number — `|a + bi| = √(a² + b²)`, so `|a + bi|² = a² + b²`

the precise `1.96 · √(p̂(1 − p̂)/n)` margin for proportions

manipulation of formula — change of subject

**substitution check** — show `g(1.5) = 0`

**interpret positions on a number line** when the weight oscillates back and forth. Compute by careful position arithmetic

**use the similarity result** (sides in proportion)

geometric meanings of three CN operations on the Argand plane — **negation** (180° rotation), **conjugation** (reflection in the real axis), and **multiplication by `i`** (90° anti-clockwise rotation)
binomial expansion at low power — either direct multiplication or the binomial theorem with `n = 3`
**savings annuity** — each deposit compounds for a known number of months. Compounding factor `1.0035` per month

differentiation — constant + power rule + trig with chain rule (the "multiply by the derivative of the angle" step)

complex division by the conjugate trick

sum of an arithmetic series; the page-22 sum-formula

evaluating a derivative through a composite — **simplify the log first using the rules of logs, THEN substitute.**
pattern recognition; identifying the "diamond" `|a| + |b| = n` rule

**standard form** of a circle: `(x − h)² + (y − k)² = r²` has centre `(h, k)` and radius `r`

angle at `B` in the isoceles triangle `BCD` — can be found via the **trig-area formula** `(1/2) ab sin C = Area`, or via cosine rule, or via splitting the isoceles triangle into two right triangles and using `arctan`

**stratified random sampling** procedure + **commentary on relevance**
